---
format:
  html
---

## Not to Be Taken Literally: Data, Statistics, and Other Common Misunderstandings {.unnumbered}

You already encounter statistics and data regularly, even when it doesn’t look like math: the “feels like” temperature on your weather app, wait times, estimated train arrivals, nutrition labels stating "per 100 g," your step count, or even personalized streaming recommendations. Even every single AI, Chatbot, or Language model are foundationally built using statistics. The critical idea isn’t that everything reduces to numbers (even though it could if we wanted it), but that numbers are always attached to situations and our lives if we like it or not. When we say “data,” these are observations but with context—knowing who or what is being observed, where, when, and how the observation was made. Without that context, a number is just decoration. Think if someone just said four when arguing.

This submodule tries to reset your understanding of what “statistics” actually means before the first week starts throwing terms at you. Statistics is a way of making sense of imperfect information by studying uncertainty—it transforms messy, incomplete observations into clear statements about patterns people can act on. It is a reasoning craft that relies heavily on thoughtful design, clear communication, and careful interpretation—supported by software but never replaced by it.

Several persistent misconceptions tend to get in the way. The first is that statistics—or for us biostats— is merely “more math” In practice, math usually runs quietly in the background; what you do is more design and reasoning. Frame questions clearly, select summaries appropriate for your data, and honestly communicate your degree of certainty. You can always do these calculations, but modern computing and software makes it more friendly. Another misconception is that statistics offers certainty. It doesn't; instead, it helps you openly express uncertainty, enabling informed decisions. You should walk away from a statistics class understanding not that statistics are rigid but flawed and always taken with some caution. There's also the misconception that statistics boils down to pushing buttons in software. Software tools are indeed useful, but without a clear research question, proper design, and reporting, they only produce confident nonsense. Many people also think “more data fixes everything.” More data can reduce random noise (We’ll look at distribution later), but it cannot fix poor definitions, biased sources, or incorrect comparisons. That’s why we hear about validation and who was the sample. Lastly, some assume statistics itself is neutral or inherently unbiased. Every choice—what to measure, who gets counted, what methods to use, and how results are shared—has discretion, and consequence. Good statistical practice involves transparently naming these choices and their limits. You must always be able to defend your choice.

If you keep only one idea from this page, make it this: statistics is the practice of asking careful questions and providing clear, honest answers in the face of uncertainty. Here, you won’t be asked to compute by hand (some numbers will come later). Instead, you'll be exposed to more foundational concepts for use later to decide your own analyses. Developing this early will help you make smarter choices both academically and professionally, long beyond your first semester.
